description: The cardinal direction toward the world's turning axis, the still point around which the sky appears to wheel. Phi coins 'nitho' and 'ronua' (south) as its two cardinal anchors; east and west follow the sun's path in 'sileta thorui' (sun's beginning, east) and 'sileta lumae' (sun's end, west).
sound_symbolism: The grounded dental 'n' plants the axis, the focused 'i' narrows to the single still point, and the wisdom-breath 'tho' opens to the wholeness of the turning sky around it. A fixed point, calmly held. Deliberately shares almost nothing with its partner 'ronua' (south), or with 'lawe'/'kuri' (left/right), so the four directions can never be confused.
grammatical_notes: A noun naming the direction. Before another noun it modifies by position: 'nitho ruela' (the north path). With prepositions: 'wea nitho' (northward, toward the north), 'lue nitho' (from the north). Value-neutral by design: north carries no sense of up, cold, or primacy — those associations are hemisphere- and map-relative, which Phi declines to encode.

pillars.peace-linguistics-practices: Phi's cardinal terms carry no hierarchy: no direction is 'up', none is primary. The compass is a circle of equals.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Navigators of every tradition found the still point of the turning sky and steered by it. 'Nitho' names that anchor — knowledge older than maps.

semantic_domains.nature: The still point of the wheeling sky
semantic_domains.spatial: Cardinal direction of the turning axis
